# Book-level distillation — complete-chord-melody

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc

O'Rourke's Complete Chord Melody is organized as a top-down, recipe-first method that begins with a working arrangement procedure on page one and only later supplies the theory and vocabulary needed to execute it. Chapter 1 (Module 1) opens with the four-step chord-melody method - pick a suitable tune, transpose it into a workable key, learn the melody on the top 2-3 strings, then add chord voicings below the melody notes - which functions as the book's operating system; every later chapter feeds inputs into one of those four steps. Chapter 2 (Module 2) builds the chord vocabulary the method indexes against, organized around the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy (Major / Minor / Dominant) and the chord collection construct that makes the dictionary lookup-able by melody note. Chapters 3-4 (Module 3) execute an end-to-end arrangement workflow, splitting it into a front half of melody-fingering, comping, and harmonic analysis (Chapter 3) and a back half that formalizes the load-bearing 'painting by numbers' rule and its remediation strategies (Chapter 4). Chapter 5 (Module 4) transitions from trio chord-melody to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility, shell voicings, and four core devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass). Chapter 6 closes Module 4 with annotated example arrangements as study material. Chapter 7 (Module 5) is the advanced reharmonization core, codifying five named substitution rules. Chapters 8-9 are appendices: the chord dictionary that backs the painting-by-numbers lookup, and a fingerstyle-technique appendix justifying the right-hand method underwriting the whole enterprise.

## The Four-Step Method as Central Thesis

The entire book is an elaboration of Chapter 1's four-step recipe, and almost every later rule is best read as a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch attached to one of those four steps. Step 1 (tune selection) is constrained by the ballad-not-bebop rule: slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier for beginners. Step 2 (transposition) is constrained by the range rule of thumb - find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets - explicitly defended against two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 (melody on the top strings) imposes a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach and is reaffirmed in Chapter 3 with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are acceptable. Step 4 (voicing the chords below the melody) is governed by the headline pedagogical rule that you don't need a chord on every melody note: chords go on long-held notes and at chord changes, exploiting the guitar's quick decay so that a chord change implies sustain rather than holding the note out. The four-step recipe is therefore not a beginner's onramp that the book later abandons - it is the load-bearing thesis the entire 250-plus pages of vocabulary, analysis, dictionary, and substitution machinery serves.

## The Painting-by-Numbers Lookup System

Chapters 3-4 supply the operational core that makes Step 4 of the four-step method mechanical rather than improvised. The linchpin construct is interval analysis: knowing each melody note's interval relative to its underlying chord is what enables dictionary lookup of a voicing with that interval on top. O'Rourke flags the most common beginner mistake - labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord - and elevates this to his explicit 'golden rule of interval analysis,' backed by the major-scale-of-the-current-chord's-root reference rule. The closed chord-tone vocabulary used as dictionary index keys is fixed: 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT (passing tone) as the escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes and an edge-case label for melody notes that anticipate the next chord. Dictionary lookup itself is keyed on three inputs - the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord - and a dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret. The painting-by-numbers rule itself is stated as: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord. The whole system is what makes the four-step method teachable rather than merely demonstrable.

## The Three Harmonic Families and the Chord Collection

Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy is that every chord belongs to one of three harmonic families - Major, Minor, or Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families, where the sub-family distinction drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The central indexing unit is the chord collection, defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note. The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I precisely because that single progression covers all three harmonic families, and collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1. On the theory side, the chapter defines a chord symbol as 'an attempt to describe a sound,' establishes stacking thirds as the core construction principle, and explains 9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th. The load-bearing pedagogical rule of Chapter 2 is the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list: 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. A final terminological rule - surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do - foreshadows the substitution machinery of Chapter 7.

## Restraint as Aesthetic Axiom

A single aesthetic commitment surfaces in every chapter and is the book's most consistent prescriptive voice: less is more. Chapter 1 states it as 'don't harmonize every note.' Chapter 4 restates it as 'less is more' (chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately) and as 'playability over ambition' (easier voicings flow musically better). Chapter 4's three remediation strategies for awkward passages all express the same axiom: cut notes out of the chord, substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads, or remove the difficult voicing entirely - which O'Rourke explicitly identifies as his own most-used escape valve. Chapter 6 sanctions rhythmic adjustment of the melody to facilitate fretboard jumps, framing the arranger's pragmatics as a rule rather than a compromise. Chapter 7's substitution machinery closes with the explicit restraint rule that subtlety beats density: keep things simple and use substitutions occasionally for color. Color itself is localized: Chapter 4 locates the 'jazzy' sound specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color, which is consistent with Chapter 8's dictionary structure (a dedicated section for altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource).

## The Solo-Guitar Extension and Its Devices

Chapter 5 extends the four-step method to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility to Step 4. The aesthetic strategy is alternation rather than simultaneity - walking bass, then chord line, then unaccompanied single line - because the listener's mind fills in the gaps to produce 'the illusion of solo guitar.' The load-bearing difference from trio playing is greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player, and a specific key-selection rule follows: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand. The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony. The solo-guitar voicing primitive is the shell voicing - root, 3rd, and 7th only - derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut, with the 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color, and a general refinement rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. The four core solo-guitar devices are defined explicitly: arpeggiation (plucking chord notes one at a time, used as a melody-space filler in slow ballads), octaves (Wes-Montgomery-style melody doubling one octave down, supported by the muting technique of resting the first finger gently on the unwanted in-between string), diads (Barney-Kessel-style parallel 3rds or 6ths as a primary melody-support layer), and walking bass (a four-step build filling beats 2 and 3 with diatonic, chromatic, or arpeggio notes between the beat-1 root and the beat-4 approach note). A chromatic approach-chord rule completes the toolkit: repeat the same voicing one fret away from the target. An intermediate drill - adding a bass note under the melody at each chord change - builds the ear before voicings get added.

## The Five Substitution Rules

Chapter 7 is the substitution-and-reharmonization core, opening with the foundational definition that a substitution simply means replacing one chord with another and then codifying five distinct named substitution rules. (1) The Tritone Substitution Rule: any chord can be replaced by one whose root is a tritone away, justified theoretically by the shared 3rd/7th guide tones that swap places between the two chords (the common-tones-enable-substitution principle that grounds the rest of the chapter). (2) The I-to-vi substitution rule and (3) the I-to-iii substitution rule, both grounded in relative-key thinking and the umbrella concept of chord addition. (4) The Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule, borrowed from Pat Martino's minor conversion concept and operationalized as substituting the iim7 from the dominant's 5th. (5) The back-cycling substitution rule, framed as movement through the circle of fourths - the most common root movement in Western music - and the melodic common-tone substitution rule that drives modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors. The chapter's characteristic pedagogical move pairs these rules with two concrete practice prescriptions: the metronome on beats 2 and 4 as the jazz-preferred form, and the load-bearing per-session quantum of just one or two bars worked slowly.

## The Fingerstyle Foundation

Chapter 9 (Appendix 2) is not peripheral - it justifies the right-hand method underwriting the entire enterprise with the claim that fingerstyle is what enables multiple simultaneous voices and therefore the whole chord-melody enterprise. It establishes the PIMAC finger-naming convention (p/i/m/a/c) used throughout the picking-pattern markings and devotes early space to ergonomic posture rules. The two canonical strokes are defined as the foundational pluck vocabulary - rest stroke (finger lands on the next string) and free stroke (finger flies free) - with the explicit preference rule that free stroke is the main technique for jazz guitar. O'Rourke's characteristic moves are tight mechanical specifications: thumb motion comes from the lowest joint with a small rowing-paddle circular motion reserved for bass notes and basslines; multi-string plucking requires the wrist rolled toward the headstock to keep thumb and index from obstructing each other. Load-bearing combinatorial rules close the chapter: the canonical two-finger pairs are p+i or i+m, the canonical three-finger groupings are pim or ima, voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand, and plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord rather than an arpeggiation. Chapter 1 had already foreshadowed this with its plectrum-bass-then-strum technique for players using a pick - the right-hand technique choice is presented as one of the method's load-bearing inputs from the start.

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the book teaches an integrated method with five interlocking commitments. First, the four-step recipe (pick tune, transpose for range, melody on top strings, voicings below) is the book's central thesis and every other chapter is a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of those four steps. Second, the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy plus the chord collection construct makes the chord vocabulary indexable by melody note, which is what makes Step 4 mechanical rather than improvised. Third, the painting-by-numbers rule - pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord - executes the lookup, governed by the golden-rule-of-interval-analysis (interval relates to the current chord, not the key) and a fixed closed vocabulary of chord-tone labels. Fourth, restraint is the aesthetic axiom that runs through every chapter: don't harmonize every note, less is more, playability over ambition, removing a voicing entirely is a sanctioned move, and substitutions are for occasional color rather than density. Fifth, solo-guitar arranging extends the trio method by adding bass responsibility on the 5th/6th strings, shell voicings as the primitive, and four named devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass), while Chapter 7's five named substitution rules supply the reharmonization toolkit and Chapter 9's fingerstyle technique supplies the right-hand foundation that makes simultaneous voices physically possible. The book's pedagogical voice throughout is procedural and permissive: rules are stated, then explicit carve-outs and escape valves are sanctioned (cut notes, substitute diads/octaves, remove the chord, rewrite the melody's rhythm), so that the four-step recipe remains executable even when its strict output isn't playable.

# Chapter 1 — Module 1 - Chord Melody Quickstart

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 lays out O'Rourke's foundational four-step chord-melody recipe that the entire course operationalizes: pick a suitable tune, transpose it if needed for range, learn the melody on the top 2 or 3 strings, then choose chord voicings below the melody notes (Four-step chord melody method). For step 1 he restricts beginners to ballads rather than bebop because slower tempos and longer-held melody notes are practically easier (Tune selection rule), and for step 2 he gives a concrete fretboard-region heuristic — find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets (Range rule of thumb) — naming the two failure modes the rule is designed to avoid: too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy (Range failure modes). The top-string canvas forces a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach (Horizontal melody fingering). The chapter's headline pedagogical rule is that you don't need a chord on every melody note (Don't harmonize every note), operationalized by placing chords on long-held notes (Place chords on long notes) and exploiting the guitar's quick decay by using a chord change to imply sustain rather than holding the note out (Sustain workaround). Load-bearing terminology introduced includes the four-step method itself, the top-2-strings constraint, and right-hand technique choice (Right-hand technique choice) — with a concrete plectrum move of plucking the bass note first then quickly strumming the rest of the chord to negotiate tricky string skips (Plectrum bass-then-strum).

# Chapter 2 — Module 2 - Chord Vocabulary

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 builds the chord vocabulary that the rest of the method indexes against, beginning with O'Rourke's three-family taxonomy — every chord belongs to Major, Minor, or Dominant (Three Harmonic Families) — then refining minor into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families that drive major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice (Minor and dominant sub-families). The central organizing unit is the chord collection, defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note so the dictionary becomes indexable by melody note (Chord collection definition), and the dictionary is structured around ii-V-I because that single progression covers all three harmonic families (Why ii V I for collections). Collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1 (2nd-string collection rationale). On the theory side the chapter defines a chord symbol as an attempt to describe a sound (Chord symbol definition), establishes stacking thirds as the core construction principle (Stacking thirds), and explains 9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th (Second-octave structures). The load-bearing pedagogical rule is the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing (Theoretical vs playable), filled by an explicit omittable-notes list — 5th, root, 11th, and 9th, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords (Omittable notes list). Final terminology: the diatonic-vs-altered distinction that drives chord-symbol notation (Extensions vs altered tensions), and the rule that surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do (Context defines chord name).

# Chapter 3 — Module 3 - Let's Create a Chord Melody Arrangement (First Steps & Harmony Analysis)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 walks the student through the front half of an actual arrangement workflow — melody fingering, comping, and harmonic analysis — before any voicing selection happens. It reaffirms the top-2-string canvas with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine (Top-2-string melody constraint), insists the arrangement be notated on paper or software so it can be iteratively refined (Write down the arrangement), and prescribes a pre-step of simply comping the song's basic chords in a band context to train the ear before voicing choices begin (Basic comping before voicing). The harmonic analysis pass has three explicit outputs: chord qualities (major/minor/dominant), key changes, and recognizable progressions like ii-V-I (Harmonic analysis goals), notated using uppercase Roman numerals for major/dominant and lowercase for minor (Roman numeral conventions). The linchpin of the whole 'painting by numbers' method is then introduced: knowing each melody note's interval relative to its underlying chord is what enables dictionary lookup of a voicing with that interval on top (Why interval analysis). O'Rourke flags the most common beginner mistake — labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord (Interval relates to current chord) — and reinforces this as his headline 'golden rule' (Golden rule of interval analysis), backed by the rule that the reference is always the major scale of the current chord's root (Major scale reference rule). Load-bearing terminology includes the closed chord-tone vocabulary used as dictionary index keys — 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7 (Chord-tone vocabulary) — plus the PT (passing tone) escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes (Passing-tone label) and the edge case where a melody note anticipating the next chord is labeled against that next chord (Melody anticipates next chord).

# Chapter 4 — Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 completes the 'painting by numbers' arranging method and addresses what to do when the rule's strict output isn't playable. Chords get placed on the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes (Where to place chords), with the single load-bearing rule being to pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord (Painting by numbers rule), softened by a carve-out that a voicing can be slightly adapted as long as the melody note ends up on top (Adapt voicing for melody). Dictionary lookup is keyed on three inputs: the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord (Three voicing-lookup inputs), and a dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret (Transpose by sliding shape). The chapter's pedagogical center of gravity is restraint: less is more, since chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately (Less is more), and playability beats ambition because easier voicings flow musically better (Playability over ambition). O'Rourke gives three remediation strategies for awkward passages — cut notes out of the chord (Three options for hard passages), substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads (Diads/octaves alternative), or remove the difficult voicing entirely, which he calls out as his own most-used escape valve (Removing chord entirely). The chapter closes by locating the 'jazzy' sound specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color (Tension from dominants).

# Chapter 5 — Module 4 - Arranging For Solo Guitar (Techniques)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 transitions from trio chord-melody to solo guitar arranging, organized around an aesthetic strategy and four core devices. The aesthetic strategy is alternation rather than simultaneity — walking bass, then chord line, then unaccompanied single line — because the listener's mind fills in the gaps to produce the illusion of a full band (Illusion of solo guitar). The load-bearing difference from trio playing is greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player to cover that role (Bass on 5th/6th strings), and a specific key-selection rule follows: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand (Open-string keys). The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony (Bass-upper proximity issue). The solo-guitar voicing primitive is the shell voicing — root, 3rd, and 7th only (Shell voicing definition) — derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut (Drop the 5th), with the 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color (6 or 7 substitution), and a general refinement rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good (Avoid doubled notes). Workflow-wise, an intermediate drill of adding a bass note under the melody at each chord change builds the ear before voicings get added (Bass-notes-at-changes drill). The chapter then defines the four core solo-guitar devices: arpeggiation as plucking chord notes one at a time (Arpeggiation definition), octaves as Wes-Montgomery-style melody doubling one octave down (Octaves definition) supported by the muting technique of resting the first finger gently on the unwanted in-between string (Octave muting technique), and diads as Barney-Kessel-style parallel 3rds or 6ths (Diads definition). It closes with a four-step recipe for walking bass — fill beats 2 and 3 with diatonic, chromatic, or arpeggio notes between the beat-1 root and the beat-4 approach note (Walking bass four-step build) — and a chromatic approach-chord rule of repeating the same voicing one fret away from the target (Chromatic approach chord).

# Chapter 6 — Module 4 - Solo Guitar Arrangement Examples

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 opens Module 4 by framing complete solo-guitar arrangements as study material, with O'Rourke's core pedagogical instruction that students should physically learn the arrangements rather than just read them, because hands-on internalization is how the underlying techniques transfer to a student's own work (Learn the arrangements hands-on). The chapter then walks through the load-bearing arranging devices visible across the example renditions: arpeggiation as a melody-space filler in slow ballads (Arpeggiation fills ballad space), diads as a primary melody-support layer (Diads support the melody), and the up-tempo stack of diads, octaves, and passing chords used together to drive faster arrangements (Up-tempo devices stack). O'Rourke's characteristic move surfaces in the explicit permission to rewrite melodic rhythm when fingering demands it: rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable to facilitate fretboard jumps (Manipulate melody rhythm for fingering), framing the arranger's pragmatics as a sanctioned rule rather than a compromise.

# Chapter 7 — Module 5 - More Advanced Arranging Techniques

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 is the substitution-and-reharmonization core of the method, opening with the foundational definition that a substitution simply means replacing one chord with another (Substitution defined) and then codifying five distinct substitution approaches as explicit rules. Tritone substitution is introduced as the most useful jazz substitution (Tritone substitution defined), justified theoretically by the shared 3rd/7th guide tones that swap places between the two chords (Guide tones explain tritone sub, Common-tones enable substitution), and formalized as the canonical Tritone Substitution Rule that any chord can be replaced by one whose root is a tritone away (Tritone Substitution Rule). Relative-key thinking grounds the next pair: the I-to-vi rule and the I-to-iii rule that license major-to-minor reharmonization via shared key signature (Relative key definition, I to vi substitution rule, I to iii substitution rule), framed under the umbrella concept of chord addition (Chord addition defined). O'Rourke then borrows Pat Martino's minor conversion concept to license minor-for-anything substitution (Minor conversion concept) and codifies a concrete Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule using the iim7 from the dominant's 5th (Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule), followed by back cycling as movement through the circle of fourths—the most common root movement in Western music (Back cycling root movement, Back cycling substitution rule)—and finally the melodic common tone substitution rule that drives modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors (Melodic common tone substitution rule). The master's characteristic pedagogical move is the explicit restraint rule that subtlety beats density: keep things simple and use substitutions occasionally for color (Don't overuse substitutions), paired with two concrete practice prescriptions—the metronome on beats 2 and 4 as the jazz-preferred form (Practice with metronome on 2 and 4) and the load-bearing per-session quantum of just one or two bars worked slowly (Practice 1-2 bars per session).

# Chapter 8 — Appendix 1 - Chord Dictionaries

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 is a reference appendix rather than instructional material, consolidating the chord voicings introduced earlier in the method into a single chord-melody dictionary keyed to Module 3 (Chord melody dictionary purpose). The load-bearing organizing taxonomy is a four-way split of ii V i voicings by quality and string set: major ii V I on string set 4321 and on string set 5432 (Major II V I string set 4321, Major II V I string set 5432), and minor ii V i on the same two string sets with the b5 and b9 alterations characteristic of the minor cadence (Minor II V I string set 4321, Minor II V I string set 5432). A second section catalogues altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource (Tensions and extensions on dominants) and then pivots to the solo-guitar voicing reference for Module 4 (Solo guitar shell voicings), built on the structural backbone of 6th-string-root shell voicings (Shell voicings root on 6th) plus a catalog of extensions stacked on top of those shells (Shell voicing extensions catalog)—the load-bearing harmonic vocabulary O'Rourke uses for solo arranging.

# Chapter 9 — Appendix 2 - Fingerstyle Training

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 is a fingerstyle-technique appendix that justifies the right-hand method underwriting the entire book, opening with the claim that fingerstyle is what enables multiple simultaneous voices and therefore the whole chord-melody enterprise (Fingerstyle plays multiple voices). It establishes the PIMAC finger-naming convention (p/i/m/a/c) used throughout the picking-pattern markings (PIMAC finger naming) and devotes early space to ergonomics: the foundational rule that hand position must promote agility from day one (Early hand position matters) and a four-rule posture catalog covering wrist curve, wrist-arm alignment, relaxation, and optional fingernails (Tips for Correct Fingerstyle Hand Placement). It then defines the two canonical strokes—rest stroke (finger lands on the next string) and free stroke (finger flies free)—as the foundational pluck vocabulary (Rest stroke vs free stroke defined), with the explicit preference rule that free stroke is the main technique for jazz guitar (Prefer free stroke for jazz). O'Rourke's characteristic moves are tight mechanical specifications: thumb motion comes from the lowest joint with a small rowing-paddle circular motion (Thumb motion mechanics) reserved for bass notes and basslines (Thumb role in fingerstyle jazz), and multi-string plucking requires the wrist rolled toward the headstock to keep thumb and index from obstructing each other (Wrist angle for multi-string plucks). Load-bearing rules close the chapter: the canonical two-finger pairs are p+i or i+m (Two-finger pluck combinations), the canonical three-finger groupings are pim or ima (Three-note pluck combinations), voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand (Four-finger maximum, skip pinky), and plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord rather than an arpeggiation (Sound multi-string notes simultaneously).

      "summary": "The book's central thesis and operating system: a four-step chord-melody recipe that drives every later chapter as either a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of its four steps. Step 1 picks a suitable tune (ballads, not bebop, since slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier). Step 2 transposes the tune into a workable key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets, avoiding the two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 learns the melody on the top 2-3 strings, imposing a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine. Step 4 voices chords below the melody using the load-bearing painting-by-numbers rule: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord, applied to the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes, since the guitar's quick decay lets a chord change imply sustain.       "name": "Three Harmonic Families Taxonomy",
      "summary": "Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy partitions the chord universe into three harmonic families - Major, Minor, and Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families. The sub-family distinction is what drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The chapter also formalizes the chord-construction principles that back the taxonomy: a chord symbol is an attempt to describe a sound, chords are built by stacking thirds, and 9/11/13 extensions are explained as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th.       "id": "greg-orourke:complete-chord-melody:voicing-construction-rules",
      "name": "Voicing Construction Rules (Omittable Notes, Shells, Density)",
      "summary": "The load-bearing instrument constraint of the method: the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list and a shell-voicing primitive. Chapter 2 establishes the omittable-notes priority - 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. Chapter 5 makes the shell voicing (root, 3rd, and 7th only) the primitive for solo-guitar arranging, derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first, with the 6th as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer color and a general rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. Bass-on-5th/6th-strings prominence is the load-bearing difference from trio playing, with bass-too-close-to-upper-harmony as the diagnostic for thin arrangements and a key-selection preference for A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass.",
